item.dc:creator: PWNRSH1P

item.title: I’m doing what makes me happy, even if it doesn’t make me the richest.
item.description: TlDr: I had a decent sized dream as a kid, but realized I’m happy without it. When I was a kid, I loved animals and I loved my mom who is a nurse. I wanted to be like her and work with animals by deciding to be a veterinary technician (basically a vet nurse). As I got older and actually started learning about what it all entailed, I started to realize that it would definitely be tougher and more stressful than kid me thought, but that’s just how jobs were, I figured. I should point out now that I am autistic and can stress easily, but I have acknowledged that there isn’t really a stress free path in life, so I kept going. Americans like me are usually told that you need to go to college to get a good job and money and all that junk, so I didn’t think I should settle for anything less. After a few more years I got excepted to a veterinary technician program at a college. Once I was finally learning everything I needed and getting hands on experience in a controlled environment, I started to wonder if this was really what I truly wanted, but I felt that I had come too far to back out now. Classes were tougher than what I was used to. I went from As and Bs to being a C average, but I kept going. While all this was going on, I was working part-time at a small animal shelter to gain some more experience for my resume and because I enjoyed it. I knew I would eventually have to leave for better opportunities, but I was staying for as long as I could. Eventually, the vet tech program had a class that required me to work at a clinic, not the shelter, to gain credits. Due to the fact that my only job experience in the field was that shelter, a shelter that was literally a remodeled house and wasn’t exactly well known, finding a job at a clinic before the semester with the class came up was difficult, however; a coworker of mine who also worked at a fancier shelter that had multiple departments, including an onsite clinic, was able to help me get a job there. The plan was long but simple: start as a kennel tech (one of the lowest positions), work hard to get promoted to the veterinary department, and then use that experience to get a better chance of getting a job at an actual clinic. I noticed the issue not too long after I started: being a kennel tech was so much more fun and less stressful than what I was learning in school. I was enjoying myself, and due to how unfair the pay in the veterinary field is in general, I was making only two dollars less than the actual vet techs in the building (and in a lot of places nearby, according to what Indeed says about vet tech salaries in the area). I was in denial for a while because I didn’t want all the money that would be the inevitable student loan debt to be for nothing, but after I was one of the lucky people to have their loans forgiven, I really started questioning if I really wanted to go through with moving up. I know those two dollars I’m not making would probably add up to something in the long run, and that paying bills and rent will be more difficult without it, but I realized that I am currently content and happy with where I am now and just simply don’t want to be “better” or go as far as I can in life because I don’t want to go farther than here, aside from wanting to save up enough to get the **** out of Texas. Sorry for the long block of text; the current version of the mobile app I’m using doesn’t seem to have an Return key to divide this into paragraphs, aside from when I divided the TlDr up there. Not sure why it only worked once, but oh well.
item.dc:creator: RedDragonDaker
